Nearly all the windows in the Spire had Arts-enhanced bars and locks, just in case I tried to jump off.
I was a prized figurehead. If I went off and died, they wouldn't have been able to endure the consequences.
In the end, the flames never reached the Spire, but I did get marinated in the smell of smoke.
I wore that smoke-soaked coat for a good half month and didn't take it off until the weather warmed.
That brings back old memories here as well.
After my grandpa and I were run out of our old home, we started to wander between some of the more affluent villages.
Most people were pretty friendly to us, as long as we hid the fact that we were Infected.
I read in novels that during the harvest season, friendly villagers would prepare bratwurst and beer for the day workers at night so that they could eat and drink to their heart's content.
(Shakes head)
The friendly villagers threw some spare change at us and shooed us out of the village before nightfall, in fear that we'd steal things.
The not-so-friendly villagers prepared some rock-hard Schwarzbrot, and pointed weapons at us, intended to make us fall in line.
One time, I was helping a villager out with some farm work.
It was really hot that day and I was dripping with sweat, so I wanted to loosen my clothes while nobody was around.
Right before I was about to take off my shirt, I heard a commotion not far away.
Grandpa rushed over and told me to run, saying that one of the workers rolled up his sleeves thinking nobody was watching, accidentally revealing the Originium crystals on his arm.
The villagers immediately threw him into an abandoned cellar and locked him up, and then set out to check everyone else, one-by-one.
If not for Grandpa, I might've also died in that cellar.
Died–– They actually kill the Infected?
They didn't need to, nor did they dare.
All they had to do was wait for them to starve, crystallize, and explode...
Hasn't there been a long-standing law to treat the Infected with dignity?
I never knew about a decree like that until I first set foot in a nomadic city.
I—
I'm so sorry...
I don't know what to say.
You have nothing to apologize for. You were also deceived by the Gräfin...
No, what I mean is, if I had been a little braver back then... If I, if I had spoken up back then, maybe you wouldn't have had to––
I'm sorry...
Really, you don't have anything to apologize for.
Just as you said, the Voice of Mundane was made by man. The blame falls on those who sought to use the Voice of Mundane, not on you.
I protected you in the manner I could...
Just like you're trying to protect me now.
You're right.
Thank you, Kreide.
Come, let's find Hibiscus and Herr Czerny... They'll know what to do, I'm sure of it.
And then, we'll have that Voice of Mundane removed from you. You can thank me properly after that.
Let's go––
Kreide?!
Kreide's condition is more or less stable. The reason he passed out most likely has to do with the poison.
We've rescued a number of poisoning patients whose signs and symptoms largely match with Kreide's.
But on the subject of Kreide's future... the Countess probably didn't lie. Overall, his condition is on the edge of critical.
So, you'll help us, won't you?
I... want to trust the two of you, I really do.
But I need proof.
Even if it's circumstantial evidence, I need some objective facts to show that the Voice of Mundane isn't baseless speculation, but a part of an imminent plot––
Sir?!
Where've you been? We've been looking all over for you!
Lies? Speculation?
Can you be a hundred percent sure that what you say is true?
What you said to Kreide... Do you know that he almost died along with those Originium slugs because of your words?!
Ebenholz, is that true...?
(Nods slightly)
Had Ebenholz not found him first, you would've never seen him again!
I'm telling you, the Voice of Mundane is real. That I swear on my life!
What do you mean?
I don't have time to explain it to you right now. I have work to do.
But, I need to...
You're among the most stubborn folk I've ever met.
Go to the lounge of the concert hall, and you'll understand everything.
Right, the concert hall!
Gertrude said she did something to Afterglow Hall to make it match with the Arts during the duet!
Alright, let's go right away–
There's also Czerny. Make sure you bring him as well, in case neither of you can convince the other.
I have to go now.
Sir, you...
Ebenholz and Hibiscus trade glances.
They are both suspicious, but they are also running out of time.
He's not here?
Mein Herr stepped out a while ago.
He left?
He's off to the hospital just outside the Afterglow, to talk treatment for the Infected who got poisoned.
What's this, Mein Herr? The hospital––
I managed to twist the arms of the largest two and got them to sign contracts, but as for the rest... there's no hope.
But Rhodes Island signed an agreement with those hospitals!
Oh, I didn't notice you were here as well.
There's no point. Rhodes Island's contracts are limited to symptoms caused by Oripathy itself. They have ample grounds to not treat Infected patients who've fallen ill from poisoning.
But, Mein Herr, you got those two hospitals. That should be enough, right?
Hmph. Enough? Of course it's enough! I put every copyright I hold down as collateral, of course the consortium behind them thinks that's enough!
Don't tell me you– You transferred all your copyrights over to that consortium?!
I did. Not just the existing copyrights. I even sold the distribution rights for tomorrow's concert to them.
Now, I can start from scratch... *cough*, *cough*.
*cough*... *cough*!
Mein Herr?!
You're awake?
Urgh... Mhm...
Mr. Czerny, you didn't take any precautionary measures when you went out, did you?
You're talking about the fumes from those Originium slugs? I didn't waste a single second getting those contracts signed. That's all that mattered to me when I left.
Promise me you'll never be that reckless ever again.
'Ever again?' Are you saying, I still have time left to live?
That's why I'm here.
Heh. You never want to see a doctor until your body gives out on you.
Thank you, Hibiscus.
I'm just doing my job.
That reminds me, why haven't you said anything, Ebenholz? Are you thinking of throwing in the towel now that the Afterglow is too dangerous?
I'll tell you straight– now that we've come this far, the concert MUST go on. Don't even think about running.
Don't even think about running, huh...
(Whispers) Do you think he'd accept it now?
(Whispers) Give it a moment...
Seems like you're not here to tell me you're quitting the concert?
No, I'm not here to tell you I'm quitting the concert.
Ebenholz, you repeat what others say when you get nervous. I've noticed it from the very beginning.
If you have something to say, just spit it out. You think there's anything I wouldn't be able to accept in the state I'm in now?
*cough* –– *cough* *cough* *cough*!!
Mr. Czerny, here, water!
*cough*, *cough* *cough*... Damn–– damn that Gertrude!
And you! How could you possibly go along with a plan like that? Curse me for thinking you had some creative talent, and this is what you use it for? Some evil plot?
It wasn't my intention––
How could it not be? Haven't you answered to that crazy woman from the very beginning?
Czerny, I still remember what you said. Even deeply-rooted hatred can still be pure music as long as it is expressed with the right technique.
I did say that, but what comes next? What are you trying to express?
To be honest, I'm already starting to find my old way of thinking quite laughable.
But what I wish to pursue... is nothing more than freedom.
––Freedom. A longing for freedom, is it?
That's right.
You've got a long way to go before you can express that using music!
This has long been a deeply-rooted experience for me. Otherwise, I would not have come to you.
Hmph.
Come now.
Where are we going?
Where else? The concert hall.
Gertrude managed to keep me out of the lounge with her little tricks last time, but we're going to get to the bottom of this.
But, your health...
Instead of sitting here popping blood vessels over him and that woman, I might as well go see what they're up to.
Ebenholz, did Gertrude ever tell you if she did anything to the lounge?
She didn't say anything.
She doesn't have to say anything.
You can already tell?
Her fingerprints are all over this place...

See that speaker right there? It shows obvious signs of having been dismantled. The same goes for the record player over there. She didn't even bother to tighten the screws–– Tsk, even the screwdriver's been left out in the open.
Just the kind of handiwork I'd expect from a noble. Hilarious.
Hibiscus, bring that record player over. Let's see what else has been tampered with besides the tonearm.
See for yourself.
Did she just install an amplifier? That's only going to cause interference between the circuits...
She already had the stylus removed. This thing's basically just an amplifier now.
Same with the speaker. The input circuit was completely removed and replaced with an amplifier... What is she trying to do putting so many amplifiers in the lounge?
She never mentioned it.
Then let's give it a try. Go up on stage and play something. That'll be the fastest way to get our answer.
Doesn't seem like anything unusual is happening?
I can't hear anything out of the ordinary either.
Go over and tell Ebenholz to apply a little bit of Arts... Only the simplest kind to disperse drowsiness, maintained strictly at the lowest level.
*cough*, *cough* *cough*, *cough*––
Czerny!
*cough*, *cough*...
Don't worry about me... I just, got some palpitations, that's all...
Ebenholz, stand over here, and try using the same Arts as before. Remember to mind the volume.
An echo?
It's a sound coming from outside.
But isn't it the sound of Ebenholz's flute?
Czerny opens his mouth, but no voice comes out.
The answer already seems clear to him.
However, the expression Hibiscus sees on his face is not one of excitement or anger, but of terror.
Pure terror.
Gertrude converted this lounge, no, the entire Afterglow Hall, into a massive amplifier – an Arts amplification device.
The amplifier stays off as long as it doesn't detect any musical Arts.
But as soon as someone casts musical Arts, the amplification device activates... and exponentially amplifies the Arts as the music spreads through Afterglow Hall.
The leakages and Resonanz of the Voice of Mundane... are no exception.
(Stares vacantly)
In other words, Originium activation will spike in all areas that are affected, meaning that everyone in Vyseheim, virtually without exception, will be infected...
And those who already have Oripathy are even less likely to be spared. After they die, their bodies will rapidly disintegrate, releasing a large amount of active Originium microparticles...
The surrounding area will be permanently poisoned, and Vyseheim will be completely cut off from the outside world––
Very sorry to interrupt your little chat, but I'm here to offer a suggestion.
Why cast Arts of wakefulness across all of Vyseheim on such a beautiful night? I often suffer from insomnia.
My apologies, but we're dealing with something very important right now...
Gertrude?!
Good evening, Czerny.
And this must be Operator Hibiscus from Rhodes Island, yes? 'tis a pleasure to meet you.
As for the young man in shock over there, why not come visit my home for something to drink? Perhaps have a nice night's rest, and return energized tomorrow?
Ger... Gertrude... To think you'd actually––
I'd actually what? Don't tell me you're going to say that I'd actually deceive you and make you murder half the city?
That can't be right.
What you're actually mad about is how I had you running in circles, and nearly got you killed without you even noticing. Right?
Show us your pride as a noble, Graf Urtica. Come on, say something.
Ebenholz, she's trying to provoke you.
There's only one thing I need to know from you right now: Why did you come here?
I came because you woke me up, why else?
I'll take that as, you heard the musical Arts rippling outwards and realized your plans had been discovered.
That's correct.
Ahh, what a pity. With just one more day, I would've enjoyed a complete success.
Don't you think you should be thinking about how to escape? Do you seriously think your plan will proceed as normal if you manage to take us out here?
Ebenholz won't help you anymore, and regardless of what you were planning for the duet, it's not going to happen now!
Really? That's not how I see it.
As long as Ebenholz and Kreide are here, my plan has not failed.
As long as I eliminate you, I can continue negotiating with Graf Urtica. He is quite familiar with the art of the noble dealings, and changing his mind is hardly an impossibility.
Most importantly, I don't think you lot can actually beat me.
If the three of you were equal in power to Graf Urtica, then I certainly would have to watch my step. But you three, aha... Pardon my rudeness.
Don't waste your breath.
Graf Urtica, why don't we both take a step back? What do you say?
Take a step back?
I dismantle these giant amplifiers, and you continue working with me. That way, only Kreide has to die. Not a bad deal considering he only has a few days to live anyway, right?
In your dreams.
That truly is a shame. Due to unfortunate circumstances, you were forced to choose the path most ill-suited for you.
If that is your heart's desire, then so be it. What a shame––
Shut up...
You'll regret this for the rest of your life.
I said, shut up!!
The time for talk is over. It seems we can only settle this with a fight.
If I win, the self-appointed guardians of the Afterglow will have to behave themselves and keep their mouths shut so I can continue negotiating with Graf Urtica.
If you win, then there's nothing more to say. A win is a win.
Take your wand out, Graf Urtica. Or do you plan to have those two Infected fight for you?
Like I said, your provocations won't get you anywhere.
Hibiscus, stay out of this. Today, I swear I'll––
Think carefully about where we are, and what's all around us!
Unless you're able to do this without any musical Arts, you'll drive all the inhabitants of Vyseheim insane before the disease ever comes!
Tsk tsk. You're a quick thinker, Fräulein Hibiscus.
Since you caught on so quickly, I suppose there's no other choice but to take it outside.
Let me get this out there first. Rid yourself of the notion that escape counts as victory. While I personally dislike roughing up innocent bystanders, I can't say the same for my underlings.
You must not have a whole lot of confidence in yourself, based on how many you brought with you.
I'm so glad to hear your sharp wit once more. I'm almost moved to tears.
Are you feeling better now, Graf Urtica?
Of course, Dame Strollo.
Let's head outside and fight to our hearts' content.
There must be nobody for you to spar with back in Urtica. Should be lonely, right?
If I did, I wouldn't be playing with an old bag like you.
After all, you've never even challenged your own brother for his title. You have to wait for him to die before you can move up. I'm guessing you've never bested him in a fight before.
Very good, very good. I'm starting to hate you more and more.
The feeling is mutual.
Down!
In your dreams!
Fancy that, you can still struggle...
I'm not holding this one back, Dame Strollo!
You—read me?!
Who knew you could bite off more than you could chew?
Unripe. You're just relying on the Voice of Mundane... Your control of Arts is still in its infancy—
Hrragh!!
Huff, *pant*, *pant*...
D—Did that get her...?
Heh, I never thought you'd be one to best me, Graf Urtica.
So you admit defeat?
Hmph. A loss is a loss. What, does not admitting it make it my victory instead?
You... Why are you unperturbed?
Because... how exactly can you lay a finger on me?
I'm the Lehnsfrau of this city. It's night, you broke into a concert hall I administrate, and you attempted to tear down legitimate renovations ratified by my own person.
You even injured me as I did my best to stop you—
Graf Urtica, Rhodes Islander, and you, Czerny. You should be the worried ones, isn't that right?
As despicable as ever, Gertrude.
Despicable? Is that how you treat your patron, your back of over a decade?
Czerny, I allowed you to use this concert hall.
You're no more than a mere Infected. Your position? I gave you that too.
Your cherished Afterglow could only limp its way to the present day on MY benevolence.
Don't forget that.
How dare—!
All of you, kindly leave.
I wouldn't be so sure.
Graf Urtica—this title carries with it disgrace, all throughout Leithanien.
But it also means that wherever I go, the Empresses' informants are sure to be.
And what if they are? I told you, you're only making vain attempts to destroy my music hall.
Even if Their Majesties' agents were to show themselves, they would arrest you by all rights, not me.
Biegler! Biegler!
You have to have witnessed this all now, right?
Do you still intend to fold your arms and watch?!
I saw the grand Graf Urtica fall to breaking and entering a concert hall, in the dead of night, with a gang. Aside from that, I saw nothing.
Did you not hear her plot?!
Do you mean Gräfin Strollo's plot, or the Graf Urtica's plot jointly schemed with her?
Why you...
I did in fact hear.
However, even if I recorded your entire dialogue just now, that doesn't prove a thing.
Evidence, Graf. Everything needs carefully selected evidence. What proves Gräfin Strollo intended to act against the Empresses?
Proof. You come to me at this moment and raise the matter of proof...
Right. The Voice of Mundane!
We discovered there was research into the Voice of Mundane in the sewers!
What?
Heh.
Seems you're not off the ball after all, Graf Urtica.
What research? I know nothing about this.
Feigning stupidity at this point's passé, Gräfin Strollo.
You may not be directly linked to the notes and plans in that sewer laboratory, but we have our ways.
With our leads there, it'll be a walk in the park to find your other little bases.
You are presently suspected of launching research into the Voice of Mundane forbidden under Their Majesties' decree. I want you to come with me.
Wait, because she was researching the Voice and nothing else?!
Let me reiterate to you again, Graf Urtica, and the rest of you.
As long as you have no clear evidence, proof your 'foul product' is going to blow up like you say it does—
Every accusation you throw at her turns into slander, nothing else.
Let me take a look at those Voice of Mundane research files! I can give you exhaustive evidence!
Very sorry, but that's classified Leithanian information. I can't hand it over to a foreign organization.
You're just wishing it out of existence!
There's a lot in these lands that doesn't go the way you want it, Fräulein.
But...!
Let it go, Hibiscus.
He's made his point very clear. Our discoveries aren't enough to convict Gräfin Strollo.
And he only showed himself because he found other evidence to do so.
I should even be thanking you for deigning to show up in the wake of my hissy fit. Isn't that right, Biegler, my dear spy?
I'll be honest, if I had to compare you and Gräfin Strollo as nobles, I'd say you win out in my books, Graf Urtica.
And you, Herr Czerny.
Hm?
We have Gräfin Strollo contained now. Even if whatever your friends are worried about is real, either way, the concert's safe.
Of course, I still recommend you cancel it. Safest for settling this.
I thought the concert tomorrow had already been forbidden.
Whether you hold it remains up to you.
Okay, Gräfin Strollo, I believe your loyalty to the Empresses. I'd just like to take you for... breakfast. You wouldn't refuse my invitation, would you?
Hah. Breakfast in these early hours? Very well.
Graf Urtica, you will regret betraying me.
I'll regret many a thing, but that is the one matter I never will.
Well, we've just let them go.
Gertrude...
You still had a word waiting for her?
If profanity counts for a word.
Kreide still hasn't woken.
Leave him with me, I'll set off straight away and bring him to the Rhodes Island landship.
You're taking him away?
I am. He'll only get the proper treatment at Rhodes Island.
I won't allow it.
Why?
Even if you can pacify his condition, what will you do about the Voice of Mundane?
I don't doubt your medical prowess, but is that something you can truly solve?
I can't promise.
But we've got no other choice now.
Don't tell me you still hope Gertrude'll help you get rid of it through ensemble?
I can only hope.
You're—
How long will it take from Vyseheim to your landship, I ask you?
You won't say. Because you know Kreide isn't likely to make it there.
But even if what Gertrude says is true, are you really going to sacrifice yourself to do this?
As long as I can do it, why shouldn't I?
Hibiscus stares, blank, for a spell.
The look in his eyes now, compared to when she first met this young man—the difference is like black and white.
He's serious, she thinks.
Fine.
Even if I help you to the best of my ability, we know too little about the Voice of Mundane, and what we do know was all told to us by the Countess. We're sifting through fact and fiction...
Looks like I made it.
Sir, you...
If you need more info about the Voice of Mundane, I have this project plan right here.
Sir, did you...?
You pulled the same thing as Biegler...
I did.
You probably imagined, but Kreide's a fin who slipped through the program's net back in the day.
How could the Empresses let such a wildcard wander freely, you know?
But you've been roaming together for more than a decade!
And that's why no one ever suspected it.
But didn't Biegler say it was classified—
Which is why I filched it from where he kept it.
If I can save Kreide, then the hell does it matter if anyone's shown this damned plan?! And it's a long-overdue damning if you ask me!
*cough* *cough*, *cough* *cough*.
C—Calm down, you're still very weak...
Let me see the plan papers, Hibiscus. You take this old fellow somewhere to rest.
I will.
Sir... does Kreide—does he know?
I never told him, but he's very smart. I'm sure he has a vague guess.
This is too cruel to him.
Cruel.
Heh.
Fräulein, do you know how I became an Infected?
No.
Surely you've heard how in the age before the Empresses' rule, Leithanien had explored classical Originium Arts to heights hardly surpassable.
Yes.
So do you know the price Leithanien paid, to reach those heights?
Countless human sacrifices, among which were huge numbers of Infected.
Yes. Infected.
During that period, some Kurfürsten were even handing over Infected to a regular quota, and they treated this as a custom.
But Infected are people too. It's not like they'd let their lives be thrown away.
So, can you guess what happened in the end?
You don't mean...?!
It looks like you have.
Just slash your arm with Originium, and now you qualify as an Infected too.
That's how mine came about.
Melody... it's melody. The Voices of the Mundane are the melodies the Witch King left behind!
I can't believe this...
No, this is the only way it makes sense!
He wasn't just Leithanien's mightiest Caster. The Witch King was also Leithanien's most feared musician.
His mastery of music was at the epitome. Some even said his insight into music was of the same grade as his Originium Arts research.
But rhythm, tonality, texture... he threw those raw elements that make up the beauty of music to the hounds. Every piece of his left in this world is filled with murder and devastation.
His opuses have long been forbidden pieces in Leithanien. If the Voice of Mundane is a string of melodies left by the Witch King—
Then the Voice is without a doubt a remnant opus of his, and you are its very extents.
We are not his opus!
You shouldn't deceive yourself. Moreover, if this is the case...
You have a solution?
I need you to perform the ensemble that repelled those Originium slugs again.
When you did that, you grasped the Voices inside yourselves, didn't you?
Ensemble? Right now?
Do you comprehend the situation we're in here?
I do, and I know it better than you do!
But—!
Let's hear Herr Czerny out, Ebenholz.
Kreide, are you alright? Did you come here alone?
Andante brought me over.
But you can't double the Voice of Mundane's melody again like you did before. It did something wrong to you last time...
I don't want to leave any regrets.
Alright... Herr Czerny, what did you think of?
If the Voice can be said to be a string of melodies, then whether those remnants who used you for their experiment, or Gertrude...
Their aim has to be to conduct the Originium Arts contained within that melody, or should I say, the melody that flows within this Originium Arts.
Which is to say, their exploration of the Voice is entirely to unearth the supposed secrets within.
In plain words, they're trying to make the Voice more 'clear,' more 'beautiful.'
Thus, there is one thing I can very definitively tell you—
There is no method that can 'transfer' the Voice anywhere in these plans.
I'm not surprised anymore.
But that's not because doing so would be too difficult. Precisely the opposite. It's because, in their eyes, it would be a waste of what they have.
Because every melody the Witch King left behind is different. Each comes from its own period, and contains its own completely separate emotions and ideas.
What sense can there be in putting two entirely different songs together?
Just as when you performed together, the further you pursue it, the more disastrous the effects become.
Even the Originium slugs fled because they felt your performance to be far too ear-bleeding.
I presume they've done similar transfer experiments before, and realized how bad the results were... then.
They did.
However, even if two pieces are diametrically opposed, a sufficient lead-in can still be enough to accomplish the transition.
Therefore, I want you to perform my Morgen und Abend.
Upon which, I want you to dig your own selves out from within the piece, just as you did that evening.
I have to feel the melody the Voice of Mundane left within you, and then, I will write you a new song.
Wait. No matter what, you need something to hold the Voice so you can transfer it.
And the experiment results said all attempts to implant two Voices in one person failed.
I'll be the vessel, is that what you mean?
Don't trust that little determination you have to be enough for self-sacrifice, boy.
I will try with all my might, through performance, to drag the Voices from the both of you.
If I cannot, I will personally serve as this receptacle, if I am able to.
Why would you go so far for us?
If I remember correctly, my Morgen und Abend was your turning point in learning flute.
And so what?
So it's likely you heard of how I came to write it. That Gertrude woman had to turn it into a blasted 'selling point' for me, though I never wanted for anyone else to know.
You lost your dearest, closest friend, and you wrote the piece in your grief.
'Grief.'
That description was far off the mark.
You should be using the term 'indignation.'
That was the first time I came to find that life would not unfold how I imagined it, and the first time I knew what music meant to me—
My rebellion against a fixed fate.
Turning ordinary civilians into Infected... That's too cruel...
I have no idea how many times I cursed the Witch King in those days, but perhaps someone heard my tirades.
However, before they could send me to the laboratories, Their Majesties had overthrown the Witch King. I was saved.
And that's why I swore to dedicate the rest of my life to Their Majesties' cause.
And so you became a spy?
Correct.
I can't tell you what I did when I was an agent, and you don't need to know.
All you need to know is that I was the agent who found the Witch King's Remains that had been researching the Voice of Mundane.
By the time we found them, they had already experimented on Kreide and failed. It's why his body is the way it is now.
You didn't plan to save him, right?
How'd you know?
From what I know, when the Twin Empresses ascended to the throne, they issued an edict that forbade all Witch King era research, and the Voice of Mundane research obviously fell into that category.
But if the Voice of Mundane is really that amazing, I can't imagine that not a single person has turned his attention to it in the twenty years since the Witch King was overthrown.
What's more... 'The Witch King left his mark within.' 'The Witch King weaved his power into the melody.' The Witch King himself is the core of these ideas.
Deep down, their pursuit of the Voice of Mundane might actually be nothing more than worshipping the Witch King himself.
And given the way that Mr. Biegler does things, I'd say a spy's principles lie with serving the Twin Empresses.
In other words, to spies like you, the Voice of Mundane doesn't further the Twin Empresses' interests in any way.
Junge Frau, until I saw how reckless you were, I took you for a fool.
And now, it seems you just didn't know how to put your wits to use.
Uhh.
You're goddamned right. Here, I brought the research plan along. There's no point in hiding any of this anymore.
The Voice of Mundane is unique. It's undoubtedly the Witch King's lingering echoes in the world, and there is definitely some power hidden within.
But the Witch King's fervent followers truly believed that the Witch King left behind his avatar within it.
The power the Voice of Mundane holds is too unique. It will never see widespread use.
At the very least, the current court casters see research on the Voice of Mundane as a futile and pointless pursuit.
And that's why the handling of Ebenholz and Kreide became a problem.
Was Ebenholz chosen because he was considered safer?
Yes, we had to announce the plan's failure, and Ebenholz was naturally the best choice as there was nothing wrong with his body.
On the contrary, as there were already problems with Kreide's body... he had no choice... Death awaited him.
But he finally got away from the Witch King's Remains! And yet he had to die... That's too cruel!
Perhaps, in your eyes, our methods are inhumane.
But once you've witnessed countless tragedies, especially ones caused by those who are not inhumane, you'll come to realize that—
You can't let your feelings get in the way of everything.
You probably think Biegler is a pretty heartless man, don't you.
Yeah, I do think so.
But what you don't know is he's lived in the Afterglow and spent time with its residents day after day for more than a dozen years. His feelings for this place is no less genuine than anyone else.
There's no one who wants to skin Gertrude alive more than him, but he can't.
Do you think you're the angriest person to ever talk to him?
Mr. Biegler... he...
Besides, Kreide's condition endangers those around him, too. He has no way to get too close to anyone, and his past destined him to never live the life of a normal man.
He truly didn't have a choice.
But you let your feelings get in the way with him. You offered him a choice other than death.
Is that really a choice? He had to wander the world with an old man who's half dead. He learned how to play the cello, and yet he rarely had a chance to even touch one.
He can't make friends. He can't even get too close with anyone. Can you really call that a proper life?
But he lived.
Perhaps.
Having seen how he trembled back then, I can still feel a sharp pain in my arms, even now.
The Witch King... The Witch King.
I, Kreide, those who got infected inadvertently, and those who were forcefully infected...

The wounds that the Witch King left on Leithanien have yet to heal, but now there are those who thirst for his return, and there are even nobles who are sheltering his remnants and facilitating their research.
Oh, how I dread...
I dread I won't live to see the day they all meet justice.
*Cough*, *cough* *cough*...
You will live.
No need to comfort me, Junge Frau.
I've lived with him for all these years. It's a miracle that I'm still alive today.
He is like a real grandson to me.
I brought him here because I was actually thinking of introducing him to Biegler and setting him up with a job as an agent.
That way, I would at least get to die knowing he's in good hands.
But now... It doesn't really to me so much anymore.
I always thought... it was my fate to give my life to Their Majesties as an agent, but it seems... that's not really all there is, heh...
Sir...
Crash.
A tremendous noise comes from inside.
Go, let this old grandpa sit here a little longer. Go help them.
If you don't feel well, be sure to call my name!
The Voice of Mundane did not have a name, at first.
The melody was abandoned by the Witch King, after all.
It was a melody improvised by him on the spur of the moment and transcribed by his court musicians.
If the Witch King's complete pieces are howls from a deep abyss, then these fragments are indeed still melodies of the mortal realm.
And thus, these fragments were named the Voice of Mundane.
Be that as it may, the Witch King's power is still immensely terrifying.
Ebenholz, I may have asked you to show your purist form in music, but now—
I want you to think back to your life the past two dozen-or-so years. Let your resentfulness pour out.
Music is a portraiture of your life. It's the steps you take, the eyes you peer into the world with!
Don't let your emotions cloud you, but don't betray your emotions either! Explore, vent, and drag your melody out from the deepest parts of your soul!
I... I found it...
Right, that's it! That's exactly what I want!
That's the melody that was carved into your brain... It's the melody of destruction, and it is a ravaging one.
And you, Kreide, I need the opposite from you. I need you to constrain yourself.
You are too unreserved. You encompass everything that you've experienced. Do you know that your kindness is your greatest weakness?
Are you reconciling? Why are you reconciling? Why should you reconcile? Who has the right to ask you to reconcile?
Everyone thinks you deserve better. The idea has crossed your mind too, hasn't it?!
You still aren't willing to change the way you think, huh?
Fine. Fine.
This is enough.
I can feel your melody too—
A void. A void that swallows everything whole.
Hngh...
Urgh...
Bear with it!
The energies of two wildly different Originium Arts begin to entangle with one another inside the room as anguished expressions start to appear on the two young men's faces.
Czerny knows it is time for him to join in.

Keep playing. Do not fret. If you feel something's wrong with your body, stop immediately.
But If you do feel something's off, and you're not the one to blame——
Then leave it to me.
Czerny sits in front of the piano, takes a deep breath, and starts playing as well.
However, when he attempts to sort out the two totally different surges of energy, he realizes just how much he overestimated his abilities.
The melody is strong, full of anger, terrifying, solemn, bloody—
Above all, it's a melody that fully immerses its listeners.
One moment, he's suddenly filled with hatred toward the Witch King. The next second, he has a heartfelt admiration for him.
One moment, he feels as though he is a newborn child, but he immediately feels the passage of time and its effects on his body. The very next second, he is as old as the hills.

He forces himself to keep his appearance intact with what little sanity he has left, but he feels as though he is walking in a boundless desert. The sandstorm is too powerful, and he cannot open his eyes.
He tries his best to shake the sand off. Then, he sees a towering wall that reaches even the sky. There is nothing on the wall. No pain, no anger, and no death.
It's a simple, unadorned wall.
At that very moment, he finally realizes. That is a wall he will never be able to overcome.
His mind is instantly filled with fear.
Gah!
Czerny coughs violently, sending blood gushing out of his mouth.
His cough is so intense that he tries to get up for a drink of water, only for him to stagger and knock down the music stand next to him.
Hey, are you alright?!
Herr Czerny!
I heard something just now! What happened?

A trifle, nothing more.
Now I see it. It's not that no one has ever attempted to do what I did. It's just that people who tried are all dead.
Ha, haha...
Hahahahahaha!
You're laughing?!
Mr. Czerny, You're bleeding out of your mouth... Let me wipe that for you.
How did you two feel just now?
When we drew out the Voice of Mundane, it felt very much like I was getting torn apart.
But after you joined in, it did feel like a lot of the pressure faded.
At the very least, this proves that my idea is workable.
But you're already...
I coughed up some blood, that's all. I know my own body better than anyone.
Let's continue.

lines.st: Czerny sits back on his bench.
He closes his eyes and begins to ponder a question.
Who is Czerny?
He answers himself: Czerny is a coward.
He once hid underneath the wings of his dear friend and teacher, thinking that he could live his whole life through music.
Yet the death of his dear friend, the torments of Oripathy, and poverty all worked together to make him realize that he is but an ordinary man.

lines.x: You will die.
I've lived this long because Gertrude still thinks I have value, and so she paid for my medication and kept me alive for a few years longer than most other Infected.
But if I can't finish this piece today, I might as well be dead.
If you don't want me to die a meaningless death, pick up your flute.

lines.st: He answers: Czerny is a man who sees music as everything.

Before he was famous, the people of the Afterglow showed nothing but grief and sorrow whenever he went outside. They had no appreciation for the beauty of music because they didn't know whether they would live to see the next day.
To make ends meet, he had no choice but to accept Gertrude's suggestion and become a spectacle for the nobles in the music halls left behind by the Witch King, like some kind of rare animal.
Life was way too complicated, and he wanted to go back inside his house.
At all those times, it was always music that kept him from running.
He has more than music, but it is with music that he expresses everything about him.

lines.x: Kreide, your performance is too weak.
But...
Don't worry about me. You don't have that right!
This is about your survival, not mine. Do not ever think it is a virtue to sacrifice yourself!
Your sacrifice is also a burden!
Scream, vent, and fight for the reward that should've been yours!
I... understand.

lines.st: Finally, he answers: Czerny is a man who resists fate.
Is music how he resists his fate, then?
No! Music is his struggle, his howl, and his song.
He is struggling for his friend who passed away before fulfilling her musical ideals.
He is howling for those who cannot speak.
He is singing for those who was born into suffering yet still have joy within their hearts.
In that case, is he performing in someone else's stead?
No! He knows how his friend was unwilling to die, he walked the streets where those who cannot speak gather, and he brought music to those who are trying to seek joy despite their sufferings.
Once again, he stands in front of that high wall—the Witch King. He knows he can never overcome this wall.

But, this time, he instead reminisces about the joy of discussing music with his dear friend, he remembers the love and respect he receives from the Afterglow's residents, and he remembers the morning sun he sees at the beginning of each day.
And that is why he says—

lines.x: Right now, I am my own opponent.
*cough*, ourgh.
Mr. Czerny, you're coughing up blood again!
(He's not listening to me at all...)
Let's go get some rest, Ebenholz.
But––
We don't need to be here anymore.
What we need to do is rest up and wait for Herr Czerny to finish his new piece.
Hibiscus, take care of him.
At the very least, I won't let him collapse before me. Now go.
Czerny's enemy is not the Witch King.
Even though the Witch King's offenses were heinously profuse, even though he disdains that monarch, he is still forced to concede that music itself stands unrelated to good or evil.
His opponent is himself, at this very moment.
He no longer tries to scale the wall so despairingly high.
He begins to perform.
Beneath his feet appears a platform. It brings him up in unbroken ascent, up through the clouds.
Though still no end to the wall can be seen, he no longer cares.
Even though he can feel the turbulent power dash about his body, and he cannot hold back further coughs of blood.
Hibiscus, to one side, is even given the illusion—
She can almost see Czerny's life flying by in accelerando, but at the same time, the score he writes rages:
Take it, take it all! Take everything of him!
Today marks the day of Czerny's death!
But Czerny's music will never die!

lines.st: And day breaks.

lines.x: *cough* *cough*, *cough*, *cough*...
It's... com... plete...
That's such a relief! You're—
The musician strains to lift himself, but his upper body crashes against the piano, and he leaves a discordant coda in the shining dawn.
Break a leg! I'll be cheering for you!
But... Even though I've been practicing for this day, I'm still not that confident...
Then there's that prophecy too, saying something about an ensemble... What if it's me...?
C'mon, you've got this! What's there to be scared of? All those who were afflicted last night have already been treated, so I'm sure we'll be fine today!
This is actually the first time I've had to sit with so many Infected. Hopefully this concert is worth my while.
Is Herr Czerny here yet?
According to the organizer, he was also affected by last night's Originium slug incident, but he did not cancel his scheduled appearance today.
Oh? That's good.
Damen und Herren, please take your seats. Herr Czerny will be here shortly.
You speak the truth, yes? You'd better not wait until after we're inside the hall to close the doors and announce that he can't make it––
I assure you that would never happen. Herr Czerny is only experiencing a mild seasonal malaise, and will arrive at Afterglow Hall soon.
That sly bastard... I knew the files couldn't have just up and disappeared.
I can deal with him later. The files aren't that big of a deal anyway, not when compared to the actual research on the Voice of Mundane.
––Is he going to the concert hall?
Forget it... He can knock himself out.
By the looks of things, espionage is difficult work.
Hmph. You should probably be worrying about yourself rather than about me, Gräfin Strollo.
Harboring the Witch King's remains in your own demesne and propping up research on the Voice of Mundane are serious crimes.
By the way, my good spy, how did my father and brother die, according to your records?
Let's stay on topic.
They are closely related to this topic.
Fifteen years ago, your father provided cover for research on the Voice of Mundane. After we found out, he was about to spill the beans on the men behind him, but was silenced.
Had you known, it would be reasonable to assume you'd act on the knowledge.
You think I'm trying to cover this up?
My views towards my father have also changed over time.
As a child, I felt like my father was the most terrifying person in the world. His will was absolute, and nobody could change his mind.
I liked running and jumping about, so my father would lock me up in my room. I liked to talk during mealtime, so my father would take away my dinner and make me talk to the wall for an hour.
Later, as I got older, my awe towards my father continued to grow unabated. I personally saw the way the lesser nobles kissed his feet, and the disdain with which he regarded them.
Even the Witch King's envoys had no choice but to speak softly when they approached the Strollo Spire, a far cry from their overbearing airs while on the ground.
That was the Lord of House Strollo, our Lehnsherr. A Kurfürst, and once the most revered of all houses.
But everything changed when the Witch King fell.
Let me be up front: you're currently in very bad shape.
As a doctor, I should be forbidding you from playing the concert.
But it doesn't look like you're trying to stop me.
I don't want the music that you've burnt your life away for to die in the dust.
Seems like you're finally learning to be flexible.
No, but I've come to realize that at times, certain goals must be prioritized over others.
I often found it ridiculous that my father was stupid enough to embrace the Witch King's rule.
He was so staggeringly stupid that he didn't even bother concealing his opinions.
Under his leadership, the Strollo family quickly lost the support of the Kurfürsten.
Our house also lost control over two nomadic cities and was forced to retreat to Vyseheim, near the homeland of the Witch King, Urtica.
Did Graf Strollo, who lorded over Vyseheim, foolishly believe that he didn't belong there?
So your father, discontent with the status quo, began to commune with the remnants of the Witch King.
Hah, that's what I thought at the time, but you aren't as naive as I was back then, my dear spy.
Your agents should know far better than I. Old Strollo was messing around with the Witch King's remains since the very beginning, wasn't he?
Oh, right. You still haven't answered my question. By your accounts, how did my brother die?
Food poisoning. That is the official report, and our conclusion.
Food poisoning? Hah.
Alright then. Tell me this. Just how much do you know about my father?
In exchange, I can tell you how my brother really died.
We don't actually use the term 'Witch King's remains', because it's too vague.
Some of the bastards who invoke the Witch King do so because they worship his power, while others covet the political capital he gained during his reign.
Your father was undoubtedly the latter.
And behind this second group is an extensive, sprawling net. Your father was just one small branch of it.
Also I hope you don't forget, Gräfin Strollo, that this is an interrogation, not a negotiation.
The only reason I'm answering any of your questions is because we were unable to determine your brother's cause of death.
Hm?
Look, it's Herr Czerny and that doctor!
Herr Czerny, are you alright?
*cough*, I'm fine.
We heard you were going to perform an all-new piece today?!
That's right.
Even though not everyone will get to hear it because of the concert hall's limited seating, I will come back and perform for everyone again.
That's great news!
*cough*...
Don't push yourself, Mr. Czerny.
The road from Czerny's house to Afterglow Hall is not long, but the man's steps are extremely sluggish.
Hibiscus isn't sure if he can even play the piano in this state.
Oripathy is a real disease, not some ethereal thing that can be defeated through willpower alone.
But at this moment, she chooses to place her faith in the musician's willpower.
Hibiscus can feel Czerny exerting every fiber of his being just to stay upright.
All she can do is support him, keeping him from losing his balance under his exertion.
The rest is his battle alone.
I've got it.
Hm?
I've got it. The name for the new piece.
I'll call it––
Hold on.
What's the matter?
Shouldn't those two be the first to know?
Heh, you've got a point.
My good spy, come here and behold the great hero of the Afterglow, our illustrious composer, Herr Czerny.
I don't know how long you've been hiding in Vyseheim, but even you must be quite familiar with him.
It's really something, a city like this, run by a house like yours, could produce such an outstanding musician.
You seem to have a high regard for Herr Czerny. It makes sense. Who could dislike a pure-spirited musician?
Even I've always liked him.
Oh, but don't get the wrong idea, it's not that kind of like. And even if it was, it'd never work out between us.
Surely you've heard about the story about him and his old friend.
I'm quite thankful that a woman named Gertrude is nowhere to be found in that story.
Hah, give me a break.
You should already know. The one who brought his music and his story to the world was me.
Without me, he would've died in a ditch somewhere long ago.
So you used the 'Infected maestro born in an Infected ghetto' trope as a gimmick and had him dance like a jester for the nobles who deigned to descend here?!
It seems this city is nearer and dearer to your heart than I imagined, my good spy.
To be honest, my original plan was hardly this compassionate. For the sake of reviving the House of Strollo, what is the dignity of a single musician?
However––
As a Leithanian noble, music was a compulsory part of my upbringing.
I must admit that even I have been awestruck by his music.
Listen, the performance has already begun.
The difference between these performances and his is like heaven and earth.
You've been avoiding the question for some time now.
What are you trying to accomplish?
Oh my, would you look at that. I just can't shut my mouth when it comes to Czerny.
Everyone around me thinks I hate him, and everyone around him knows that he hates me. This unfortunate misunderstanding has robbed me of a conversation partner.
But let's not talk about me anymore... Back to my brother.

Ugh, that topic really isn't as interesting as Czerny though.
But it's only natural for his death to remain a mystery to you.
It was my work, after all.
Phew... How are you feeling?
I think we've pretty much been in sync with each other.
Even though he composed this piece for the two of us, it clearly has his style all over it. I don't think it's inferior to 'Morgen und Abend' in any way.
And he only spent a single night on this... What an incredible man.
Composing is the release of a long build-up. I think he simply found the right opportunity for it.
What are you staring at?
I had no idea you knew how to give a compliment.
It's not as if I've ever downplayed his musical accomplishments.
Haha.
My only hope is that he will make it to the concert.
He'll be here.
Composing a piece of music is only the first step. Only through turning the score into actual music can the piece truly be completed.
News has already gotten out that he'll be changing the finale. He's not going to give up now.
I hope so.
Oh right, Ebenholz. Do you think this suit looks good on me? I've never worn anything this expensive before, so I don't know if I might've messed something up.
It... looks very good.
Thank goodness.
Oh, there's one other thing.
Why are you so unusually chatty today?
Is that to say I have a normal level of chatty under normal circumstances?
Yes... something like that.
Just deal with it then. Who knows, this might be the last time we ever play together.
Why would you think that?
Why wouldn't I?
After this is over, you'll return to being Graf Urtica, and I... will continue to wander.
Even though our meeting was somewhat engineered by the Gräfin, it was still just a coincidence at first, wasn't it?
But––
But we're friends now. I know that's what you were going to say.
Right.
Sometimes, even friends have to go their separate ways.
Maybe this time, we won't see each other for a long, long time.
But do you remember what I said? We can always give gifts to remember each other by.
I'll give you one of my dice then.
Aren't those your weapons? Do they still work if you're missing one?
To be honest, I'm not entirely sure.
Let's not worry about it then. I think a coin with a hole drilled through it works best, just like I said before.
What about you then? What are you going to give me?
I'm still thinking.
Take your time. As long as things go well today, that's something we'll have plenty of.
Right, we still have plenty of time.
Enough of your jokes, Gertrude Strollo!
Do you have any idea what you've just said?!
I said, I was the one killed my brother.
You've gone mad.
Mad? No, think about it carefully, my good spy.
Your once exalted father falls from grace, funding some shady research in secret. He tries to blow hot and cold, his plans are exposed, and he ultimately meets an untimely end.
Afterwards, your brother is groomed by 'them', terrified into obedience.
But he is too incompetent, he can't do a single thing right. He slips up once and almost exposes the ones pulling his strings.
Instead of watching him suffer in torment at the hands of those who always despised him, isn't it better to use him as a stepping stone so you can succeed him?
Who are 'they'? Who are the ones pulling his strings?
Oh, I would like to know that more than anyone, far more than you would.
If you had that information, I would sit down and strike a deal with you here and now.
I might even go down into the streets myself to yell about how I have information on the Witch King's remains.
Tainted witness. If I recall, this saying has grown quite popular in Columbia?
I've met no shortage of idiots who thought they could play ball with us, Gräfin Strollo.
Didn't end well for any of them.
Herr Czerny, are you alright?
I want to say yes, but that would be a lie.
If you really can't handle it, then––
Ebenholz, don't forget whose decision this whole thing was.
If that's what you want, then I will give my all.
The two of us have already mastered the piece you wrote. If you, the composer, can't perform on par due to health problems, then that's entirely on you.
Well spoken. The melody is already etched into my head. All you two have to do is follow me.
You talk a big game.
Alright. Now that Mr. Czerny is here, let's hurry up and get ready to go onstage!
Whether for our own sake or for our audience, we've all got to put our best foot forward.
Kreide, is it just me, or do you seem... unusually excited today?
Do I?
That might be because...
For the first time, I'm experiencing what it means to be alive.
I don't get you at all.
Herr Czerny, you're up.
Oh right, there's one other thing, Herr Czerny.
What is this piece called? You still haven't given it a name.

I came up with it on the way here.
I will name it——
'Licht und Schatten.'
Next up... Czerny, Ebenholz, and Kreide will ferry you away to tonight's finale.
As you may already know, this piece is Herr Czerny's new composition, debuting tonight for the very first time.
Its title is–– 'Trio for Flute, Cello, and Piano in B-flat minor - Licht und Schatten.'
The three bow to the audience, and the concert-goers in the auditorium respond with warm applause.
The music begins.
After just a few short measures, the entire audience, from the auditorium to the balcony, sits up straight in their seats, those who previously wore expressions of expectation and suspicion alike.
No whispers, no coughs, not even the sound of clothes rubbing together.
Not a single soul wishes to disturb this fleeting, unadulterated beauty.
Small beads of sweat begin to emerge on Czerny's forehead.
But this does not indicate any problems with the trio, outside of their music. It's just that the power he is required to guide and control is too precise.
They've begun their performance.
Czerny, I just knew you wouldn't disappoint me. You will find a way to help them.
Listen, this new piece... How could the audience possibly comprehend that this opening came straight from the Witch King?
Of course they cannot comprehend, because this piece already belongs to you.
Ah, this melody... It all makes sense now.
You intend to rip the Voice of Mundane out of both of them. Bold.
However, what will you do after that? Are you going to become the new vessel?
In Biegler's eyes, Gertrude has gone completely insane.
She mumbles to herself in the direction of the concert hall, as if conversing with a dear friend.
My dear spy, would you like to hear my performance?
Gertrude seems to be addressing Biegler, but is also muttering to herself at the same time. As she speaks, she walks toward the harp sitting in the corner of the room.
My patience is running out, Gräfin Strollo.
I bought this house for a reason.
It sits outside the Afterglow, but you can still hear the music coming from the concert hall clearly. A wonderful location, isn't it?
I love listening to the music that passes through here.
This way, he doesn't have to see me, and I don't have to see him.
She begins to play as she speaks.
Gräfin Strollo, the Arts Units in this building have already been disassembled.
And even if they weren't, don't think your Arts will work on me. Men easily swayed by music don't make good spies.
I only want to play the harp, my good spy.
Czerny hates me. He hates me for not treating music with purity. He hates me for using it as a tool.
I once sought to defend myself and explain it away, but I later came to realize, I might actually be exactly the woman he says I am.
Even if he carved my heart out, I would never able to treat music with purity.
Tell me, how could one who has committed fratricide; a woman who, for over ten years, has been hell-bent on exacting revenge on those puppetmasters, possibly achieve anything close to purity?
As much as Biegler doesn't want to admit it, Gertrude's performance is excellent, not at all paling in comparison to the echoes drifting in from beyond the window.
Without a doubt, this is the level that all outstanding Leithanian nobles should aspire to.
But, the words that come out of Gertrude's mouth make him shudder.
Wait, revenge?!
You're a clever man. You should've guessed that my tragic failure last night was not the result of impetuousness.
But you don't know what I'm hiding. You believe I've exhausted my plans, which is the only reason you're here chatting with me.
The truth is, I do want to buy time, but some words have been festering in my head for far too long, which is why I can't help but ramble on.
I do hope you pardon my rudeness.
Furthermore, I must apologize that you'll be dying here with me.
What the hell are you doing?!
It truly is an eye-opening piece of music – or, so it was at the beginning.
Just as the title implies, the music weaves a shattered illusion from the very beginning, making one's heart sink to the bottom of a valley as if dark clouds are blotting out the sun.

However, the tune changes almost immediately, the sonorous cello uplifting the flute's clarity with the piano behind them both to break through the layers of gloom, singing a paean to the joy of glimpsing the light of day.
O, Light!
This is the singular thought in everyone's head.
They immediately understand the 'Licht' in 'Licht und Schatten.'
They begin to look forward to the 'Schatten.'
However, a feeling of unease begins to set in to some members of the audience.
Because they see the expressions of suffering creeping across the faces of the three performers.
As if something is gnawing away at them from within.
Beautiful.
An exemplary musician, devoting his life to fighting the power left behind by the Witch King.
Just as he is about to pull this doomed battle back to a stalemate, he loses not to the Witch King, or to himself, but to the obsessions of an old acquaintance.
Hey, what are you going on about?
Do you hear the harp's voice?
Huh? A harp? Why would there be a harp? Isn't this supposed to be a trio?
Philistine.
Listen to the sound coming from that small building in the distance.
The shrill laughter erupting from the depths of despair is equally beautiful.
As for you, my former student...
While I was teaching you to speak using the violin, I heard the motif of your fate... What a cruel melody it was.
But I never expected that you could develop it into such a brilliant opus.
I salute all of you, people I may or may not know.
I shall listen. For that is the only way I know to pay my respects.
I was the one who restarted the Voice of Mundane program, my good spy.
You alone?
First, the Voice of Mundane is exceedingly rare. And after that ordeal, you ultimately took control of two of them.
Second, past research has proven that an individual who isn't blood-related to the Witch King cannot possibly withstand this type of experimentation.
In other words, even after you people tore apart that plan, it was not completely abandoned, merely shelved.
In the end, the Voice of Mundane program originates from the worship of the Witch King, but how many are there who, from the depths of their hearts, can truly worship fear itself?
They only wanted a weapon to overthrow the Twin Empresses, just as the Twin Empresses overthrew the Witch King.
As for me, after I realized that my brother's incompetence would bring ruin to our entire house, I had no choice but to act.
Nevertheless, I still never found a way to show my value to those shadowy worshipers.
Music, Arts, money, authority, even my body... Nothing worked.
Finally, I found these dust-covered plans in a pile of my father's old writings.
The reboot of the program brought a fresh breath of opportunity for me. They began to think that I might amount to something.
But what they didn't know is I've already had enough.
I don't want to be at their beck and call anymore.
So, I set this plan into motion. Not for the sake of overthrowing anyone, unfortunately.
I just want them to get a taste of what it's like to suffer.
I sucked up to them and invited them to come appreciate the fruits of my research.
If you go take a walk around the concert hall now, you might find proof of some things you've long suspected.
Everything from the human experimentation facility in the sewers to the dossier on the Voice of Mundane program... Did you deliberately allow me and Ebenholz to find them?
Those were only meant for the eyes of you spies. I never expected Graf Urtica to go looking for you people.
And you tipped your hand to keep them from discovering what you had really done to the concert hall.
Of course. The amplifiers are nothing more than a small distraction.
With those amplifiers, all of the Afterglow would have been made to suffer.
With the amplifiers dismantled, the Afterglow is indeed spared, but the same cannot be said for those inside the concert hall.
The remodeling of the concert hall began after my research into the Voice of Mundane had progressed to its next phase.
Czerny is trying to use his own composition to draw the Voice of Mundane out of the boys––
But what I'm trying to do is much simpler. The moment they try to pull out the Voice, they will realize that nothing is in their control.

I have tampered with the melody and Resonanz of the Voice of Mundane. Anyone who tries to become the new vessel, regardless of who it might be, will have to endure turmoil and chaos a hundred times greater.
The Voice, unable to find a suitable vessel, will collapse into an all-consuming void within the concert hall built by the Witch King himself, swallowing everyone within.
Of course, I am a part of this performance, and I equally have no hope of escape.
As for you, my unfortunate spy, if you don't take this chance to escape, then you can only bemoan your ill fortune.
My, what a fierce look you have in your eyes. I know what you want to do.
But my apologies. I can no longer extricate myself from this song.
Killing me would be no different from killing the three men on that stage. All it'll do is bring about those deaths a little faster.
Czerny desperately wants to stop the performance.
But he cannot do it.
The moment he begins to reel in the Voice of Mundane, he feels a single strand of abnormality. But in that solitary moment, it is already too late.
The concert hall begins to absorb and reflect their performance like a massive Arts Unit.
Resonanz. True resonance.
He immediately realizes that Gertrude's alterations were far greater than just some simple amplifiers.
The concert hall itself has become a weapon.
Why did you do this, Gertrude...?
Herr Czerny, if we continue on like this...
Don't stop!
The Voice of Mundane has already destabilized due to the interference of the concert hall.
If we stop now, not only will both of you, but everyone in the venue will die along with you when Voice rampages out of control after losing its vessels!
(But––)
They all know that this piece begins with Ebenholz's Voice of Mundane and ends with Kreide's.
Even if they don't stop, Kreide's Voice will be pulled from his body by the piece's conclusion, and the inevitable will still transpire.
This is a dead end.
There's an atmosphere of uneasiness, and they're holding back on their Arts. What in blazes is going on...?
Excuse me!
You're the one from Rhodes Island...
Hurry and start evacuating the concert hall!
Huh?
Something bad's about to happen!
If we don't hurry, it's going to be a disaster!
Is this the product of Gertrude's research? Fascinating.
What do you think about this?
Not bad for a minor Gräfin, as far as I'm concerned.
Let's have her pay us a visit afterwards.
Indeed.
How were you so sure that they'd use the concert hall today?
My good spy, you're mistaken about one thing.
I can't say for sure what is about to unfold.
What I can say for sure is – as long as they're still in that concert hall trying to save Kreide, then I've won.
Kreide's disease is my final bargaining chip. If they can somehow resolve that, then I've lost.
This is the situation.
What... the hell is this? This plan... can hardly even be called a plan!
That's because my plan was a failure from the very beginning!
You think I'm sitting here chatting with you because I have an elaborate backup plan? How's would that make any sense?

If not for Czerny's unwillingness to cooperate with me, if not for the fact that even the insignificant little Graf Urtica insists on rebelling against his trifling, worthless fate, why would I even bet on this method?
Czerny will never realize how fortunate he is. That he was born with enough talent to be recognized, that I was willing to provide him a stage.
All he had to do was be a little nicer to me, and I would've elevated his music to the loftiest heights, perhaps even before Their Majesties.
But he refused to do even that!
And that Graf Urtica. He deemed himself a vase sitting on display in his demesne, as if a bit of ridicule and insult is the greatest humiliation one can suffer in life.
But look at me. I've endured the manipulation of the puppetmasters of this city for fifteen years. Fifteen long years!
If I can't produce results, I will be silenced. By what right are they allowed to live while I am not?
I don't care about the Witch King. I don't care about the Twin Empresses. I've only ever wanted to survive!
From the beginning, all I wanted was to live on...
After coming to Vyseheim, Ebenholz settled on a few more things he wanted to do.
He wanted to spend a bit more time walking around the Afterglow, taking in the sights of the vibrant district.
He wanted to perform on a few more instruments, for the first time experiencing the charm of music.
He wanted to apologize to Czerny, to tell him how much strength his music had brought him. He wanted Czerny to become his formal music teacher, should he be willing.
He wanted to spend more time with Kreide.
He didn't know what he wanted to do with that time. Maybe chat, maybe argue, maybe even fight? He didn't know what he wanted to do with the time, but he wanted a little more of it.
But had he never come to this city, had he never accepted Gertrude's invitation, none of this would have ever happened.
It is his shallowness that has caused things to deteriorate to this extent.
He knows how to break free from this dead end, and he is determined to do it.
Frankly, had Czerny not devised a new plan, he might've even wanted to carry out Gertrude's plan.
But hypothetically, if the past him had wanted to do this, it would've only been to lessen the weight of sin within his heart.
Now, however, he will say that it is not for atonement.
He shoots a glance at Czerny and at Kreide.
One, an eminent maestro. The other, his dear, new friend.
He thinks to himself, they deserve to live much more than he does.
What he does not realize is that the moment he opened himself up completely to the music, he was no longer able to hide his emotions.
Ebenholz, don't do anything stupid!
Hah, Graf Urtica, my dear. You are this piece's beginning.
Even if you forcibly reverse this piece and incorporate it into your own mind, how will you deal with the dissonance and contradiction?
Struggle to your dying breath!
One by one, they all want to flee. They all want to flee their fates – but how, and why?
For fifteen years, I've lived in their shadows, but at last, at long last, I have won!
I've won! Fate is on my side!
None of you will escape! Not a single one!
How unsightly. The musicians seem to be fighting against the very music they play.
Did Gertrude also arrange this?
This–– I'm not so sure.
A situation seems to have arisen during our performance. Please exit the auditorium.
Once again, please exit the auditorium.
Ah, the MC just asked the audience to leave. Look––
Shut him up.
Indeed.
Urgh...
What are you doing?!
Just shutting up an Infected.
Do you have any idea what's going on?
All I know is if you don't shut up as well, you're going to end up the same way.
Don't do it!
(Even if I can only buy you a little bit of time...)
Should you ask Kreide whether he wants to break free of his old life...
His answer would surely be – 'Yes.'
His grandpa told him that his body was extremely special, something he gradually discovered himself.
The people around him would start to suffer, and those he treated with kindness would leave him in the end – so he started to distance himself from them.
But, he doesn't find it a terrible existence.
The first thing he learned during his long life of vagrancy was that cursing fate doesn't make life any better.
He can only find the meaning of his life from the few things he can do in the moment.
And now, he realizes that it is his cue.
No life is worth more than any other, but he has made friends and received camaraderie. He feels fulfilled.
The sunlight shines warmly outside. It is a good day to die.
Hm?
What––?!
The cello... Kreide! What is he doing?!
Kreide, not you too...!
Herr Czerny, how could the music you've poured your life into be trampled like this?
Besides, I am the end of the piece. It's only fitting that I do this, right?
Do you have any idea what you're doing?!
Ebenholz, don't stop. Just keep matching my rhythm.
(But then, you'll––)
Let's put the finishing touches on our final performance together, okay?
(No!)
Trust me, Ebenholz, Herr Czerny. I'll take care of this.
(I believe in you.)
The piece... has gone back to normal?
No, something's wrong! Kreide is...
Mein Herr, something isn't quite right with this Infected woman. Look here...
Forget it. Why bother with a common Infected? The appreciation of music should come first.
Listen, everything's gone back to normal now.
Seems like whatever happened before was just some shock value for the show.
I assume she wanted to demonstrate the difficulty of controlling the Voice of Mundane, for the purpose of increasing her own value.
Ah, you're right. Maybe she's not half bad after all.
Not only did she prepare such an interesting technique for us, she even treated us to an elegant piece of music.
The juxtaposition of light and shadow is vividly reflected in the trio of flute, cello, and piano.
Finally, the piece approaches its coda with an ensemble of cello and piano.
Listeners strain their ears, expecting to hear the whispers of the cold night, the embrace of darkness, the call of the abyss.
But they are greeted only with a long, drawn-out sigh from the void.
After what seems like an eternity, applause finally breaks out across the audience that gradually swells with enthusiasm.
But before the applause can sweep across the venue, it crashes against the dissonance of horrified screams.
It's over... Kreide, you've done it!
No, there's... still something I have to do...
Kreide, what's happening to you... your body?!
Ebenholz, let's go outside.
Herr Czerny, please take Hibiscus and evacuate the audience. Have them get as far away from here as they can.
You—
Kreide, your body...
I'm sorry, Herr Czerny. Fräulein Hibiscus.
There's so much I want to say to you, but... It seems like now isn't the time.
Take me there, Ebenholz, while I can still control myself.
The melody in my head... is getting louder and louder...
The two melodies are... tugging, inside my head...
Hurry, Ebenholz. Take me outside.
The concert hall is amplifying both melodies at once. I can't hold on for much longer...
I know!
Ebenholz, you...
I'll take care of Kreide.
But––
I said, I'll take care of Kreide!
Hibiscus, go with Herr Czerny and evacuate the crowd!
Upon witnessing the metamorphosis taking place on stage, the people in the auditorium begin to flee.

Panicked screams follow one after another, and Hibiscus and Czerny's shouts can be heard among them. However, to Ebenholz's ears, everything is blurring into white noise. His eyes fixate only on the plaza outside the concert hall.
You were planning to do this the entire time, weren't you?
Perish the thought...
I just... did it on a hunch.
A hunch?! When I said you were being unusually easygoing today, it was all because of a hunch?!
No, no... I truly am glad that I got to perform with you and Herr Czerny.
This is the happiest I've been my entire life.
Then why'd you go and pull a stunt like this?!
There are still so many happy things waiting for you––
But if I didn't, then either you or Herr Czerny would've done it, right?
So that means it somehow falls upon you?!
You've never had a single good day your entire life, whereas I'm the one who's had enough of living since a long time ago. It should've been me!
Sorry, Ebenholz.
Why are you apologizing... What do you have to be sorry for?!
The way I look now... must be truly hideous, right?
I've torn up the clothes you had made for me, haven't I? Those nice, expensive clothes...
Don't worry about that! I'll buy as many suits as you want!
I'm sorry––
You...
You liar...
I'm furious with you now, Kreide.
I'm really, really mad!
Kreide is too weak to lift his head, but he can hear Ebenholz's sobbing.
What's going on? What the hell just happened?
No idea... But Herr Czerny said the concert's on hold, and that Hibiscus girl was desperately urging us to evacuate.
It'll be fine though. Nothing's going to happen to us.
But the sky suddenly went dark... Do you think some disaster might actually be coming?
Think about what that prophecy said... Fades Finale's Ensemble...
Don't worry, it'll be fine. That prophecy also called Hibiscus a 'Fiend.'
It's starting to rain?
See, there's nothing to be scared of. It's just a bit of rain.
We're here.
Feel any better?
I don't think so...
I only... managed to forcibly suppress that sensation.
What do I need to do now? Hurry and tell me––
I want to... ask you a few questions.
Go ahead! Ask me anything you want!
Ebenholz, do you think... that your fate is tragic?
Is my fate... tragic?
I used to think so.
But after I met you, I realized that the past me was just crying bloody murder over nothing.
That's not your fault.
They shackled you, stole your freedom, blindfolded your eyes, and plugged your ears... That's not your fault.
Then, do you think my fate is tragic?
The first time I heard your life story, it really did come as a huge shock to me.
But I've gradually learned that your life isn't tragic. In fact, it seems very... fulfilling.
Haha, that's great to hear.
*cough*, ourgh...
Kreide, you...
I can't hold it back any longer. But, let me finish, Ebenholz.
The Voice of Mundane's completed melody swirls around in my head, and the Witch King's residual power howls from within me. Cruel thoughts are tearing at my chest, forcing me to bow down to fate.
They're forcing me to say that our stories were painful, our reunion was misfortune, and that our fates are tragic.
But!
But, Ebenholz!
I will rebuke them!
Do you understand, Ebenholz?
I won't deny that the Voice of Mundane has caused us untold suffering.
I won't deny that your life as a Graf, filled with humiliation and scorn, was a misfortune.
I won't deny that my experiences living an unstable life of wandering have been tragic.
But I will deny... I will always deny... the suggestion that my life is defined by those words.
My grandpa carried a burden like me around for over ten years. During those years, I lived in abject poverty, denied a relationship with anyone but Grandpa.
But as long as I give kindness to others, I can feel their kindness in return.
I even met a teacher who was willing to teach me how to play the cello.
Most importantly – after more than ten years, I met you again.
Ebenholz, don't ever forget. We are both subjects of that cruel, inhumane experiment. And we survived.
When we feel like cursing our own fates, we cannot afford to forget that!
We met each other again, became friends again, and delivered a perfect performance on stage together!
Are we not lucky?
We've already been blessed... Actually, is there anyone out there luckier than us? No, and there never will be!
I understand. I get it.
No, you still don't get it!
Kreide's voice is already laced with a deep rumbling. The growl of a monster.
But to Ebenholz, even those words are gentle, like words of advice from an older brother to his younger sibling before a long trip.
If you really get it, then please don't weep for me. Ebenholz, my dearest brother.
We were supposed to die together here, to bury everyone in the entire concert hall along with us.
But we fought it!
To others, this resistance might seem insignificant. But it was a chance for us to give everything for each other.
This was a sacrifice that both of us were willing to make. It just happened to be me who did it in the end, that's all!
What a wonderful opportunity. To give everything for each other, asking nothing in return!
We walk the path of rebellion against fate, just like Herr Czerny did when he poured his life into composing this piece for us––
So, stand up and hold your head high, Ebenholz.
There's still one last thing for you to do, and that is––
To use everything you have to defeat me. To keep me from destroying everything here. Do you understand?
...I do.
I really... like that look in your eyes.
Kreide unsteadily shambles to the center of the plaza.
With each step he takes, Originium crystals erupt from his body, his flesh and blood beginning to churn.
A strange melody lingers over the plaza.
The crystals rapidly envelop Kreide's body, transforming into armor, transforming into instruments.
In a heartbeat, the being once known as Kreide is swallowed up, and in his place stands a monstrosity.
Anyone who sees this monster has three words flash into his mind––
The Witch King.
We stand at a critical juncture, my exalted Damen und Herren, my dear Infected brothers and sisters.
And you too, Ebenholz!
Harken! Listen well to my final piece!
Castigate me. Lament for me. Cry for me. Sing for me.
Because today, I shall die, and you––
Will be reborn!
Stupid, foolish boy...
You didn't have to do this.
Why would you go so far for a world that owes you so much?
Sir?!
You must leave now! Originium activity is rising rapidly in this area!
I want to see my grandson one last time.
Only a little further, Hibiscus. Distract him...
Kreide, grandpa's here.
You...
Kreide, my boy.
Vent whatever anger you have on me.
Come to me, before you do any more harm!
I've deceived you for so many years... I thought about dying for your sake, but I never thought you would come to an end like this.
If your choice is to let the others live, why not let me come with you in my last days?
Let me join you on your final journey, my boy!
The crystalline monster no longer understands the old man's words.
The flash of lucidity was a fleeting miracle. All the monster can think about now is dragging everything around it into emptiness, dancing to his cello's tune.
The monster follows the sound, playing a song of death.
Sir?!
Young lady, why do you—
I'll hold down the fort here, Ebenholz. Now!
Ha!
How?!
Friendship, dedication, sacrifice—such childish ideals should have been shattered upon contact with reality. How? How?!
It's not over yet!
Kreide may have forcibly assimilated two verses of the Voice of Mundane, but he could not overcome the disorder and madness of their amalgamation!
Kreide's melody has faded, but Ebenholz's Voice lingers. I—
Argh!
Enough, Gräfin Strollo.
